front: What does the OSI session layer coordinate?
back: Data exchange between two communicating processes

front: What does the session layer establish, maintain, and synchronize?
back: The interaction session between communicating processes

front: In the session layer, what is dialogue control?
back: Managing whether communication is half-duplex or full-duplex

front: What does duplex describe in communication?
What is full-duplex communication?
back: Two-way communication at the same time

front: What is half-duplex communication?
back: Two-way communication, but only one direction at a time

front: What is simplex communication?
In the session layer, what is synchronization used for?
back: Creating checkpoints so transfer can resume from the last checkpoint

front: How do session-layer checkpoints improve recovery after a failure?
back: They avoid restarting the whole transfer

front: What does the OSI presentation layer format data for?
Which OSI layer handles syntax and semantics of exchanged information?
In the presentation layer, what does translation do?
back: Converts between machine-specific and generic character sets

front: Why might the presentation layer encrypt data?
back: To prevent plaintext reading by unauthorized parties

front: Why might the presentation layer compress data before transmission?
back: To reduce data size and network load

front: What is the OSI application layer?
back: The interface between users or software and the network

front: Which OSI layer do people usually interact with through network programs?
What application-layer protocol sends email?
What application-layer protocol provides secure remote login?
What application-layer protocol provides insecure remote login?
What application-layer protocol transfers files?
What is the secure version of FTP?
What application-layer protocol supports the World Wide Web?
What is the secure version of HTTP?
What application-layer service translates human-readable names to IP addresses?
What application-layer protocol automatically configures host network settings?
In the client-server model, communication over the internet is considered to occur between what?
What is a client in the client-server model?
back: An application that requests information or resources

front: What is a server in the client-server model?
back: An application that provides information or resources

front: Where is a client usually running in the client-server model?
Where is a server usually running in the client-server model?
How many clients can access one server?
When does a client usually run?
How often is a server usually running?
What does DNS do?
back: Resolves domain names to numerical IP addresses

front: Why do people use DNS names instead of IP addresses?
back: Names are easier for humans to remember

front: Why do computers and routers use IP addresses instead of DNS names?
back: IP addresses are easier for machines to route

front: How are DNS names structured?
In what direction are DNS names read for hierarchy?
In `finance.griffith.edu.au`, which part identifies the machine?
In `finance.griffith.edu.au`, which part identifies the organization?
In `finance.griffith.edu.au`, which part identifies the organization type?
In `finance.griffith.edu.au`, which part identifies the country code?
What kind of database is DNS based on?
back: A distributed database of names and addresses

front: What does a local DNS server do if it cannot resolve a name?
What does DHCP stand for?
What does DHCP automatically configure?
What network setting identifies a host on a TCP/IP network?
What DHCP-provided setting identifies which network a host is on?
What DHCP-provided setting lets a host leave its local network?
What DHCP-provided setting lets a host resolve names?
In DHCP, what happens in fixed-address mode?
back: A host receives the same configured values each time

front: In DHCP, what happens in dynamic-address mode?
back: A host receives the next available IP address

front: In DHCP, what happens to a dynamic IP address after the host leaves?
back: It returns to the address pool

front: Why is DHCP useful on large or public networks?
What is a VPN?
back: A way to use the internet as if connected to a private network

front: Why do organizations use VPNs?
back: To protect private data over the public internet

front: What does a VPN use to protect data in transit?
What does a VPN make packets seem to travel through?
back: The protected environment of the organization's private network

front: What does SMTP stand for?
What port does SMTP use?
What does MIME stand for?
Why is MIME needed with SMTP?
back: To encode non-basic data types for email

front: What protocol is preferred over Telnet for remote login?
Why is SSH preferred over Telnet?
What does Telnet send over the network?
What does FTP separate into two streams?
Why does FTP separate control and data streams?
back: To allow transfer control such as cancelling operations

front: What is the most common content format on the web?
What does HTML stand for?
Which OSI layer is responsible for end-to-end delivery between software processes?
What does the transport layer deliver reliably?
back: The entire message from source process to destination process

front: What is transport-layer segmentation?
back: Breaking a message into smaller packets

front: What is transport-layer reassembly?
back: Putting packets back together at the destination

front: Why does the transport layer number packets?
back: So the message can be reconstructed correctly

front: What is service point addressing?
back: Using port numbers to identify communicating programs

front: At the transport layer, what does a port number identify?
back: A program or process on a machine

front: What does an IP address identify?
Which OSI layer handles IP addresses?
What kind of error control does the transport layer provide?
back: End-to-end error control between sender and receiver

front: How does data-link-layer error control differ from transport-layer error control?
back: Data link checks neighbor-to-neighbor; transport checks end-to-end
